data.title: Ceragon Networks: A Top International Stock Pick
data.description:
Introduction

Ceragon Networks Ltd. (NASDAQ:CRNT) is a play on 5G and other communication technologies, generating wireless products and services that support wireless and cellular service providers. It is based in Israel, listed in the US, and does business around the globe.

For full disclosure, and in the interests of transparency, we first took a position in the name in late 2022. It was later our 2024 top pick. That call worked out well. Last year, the stock rallied 116.2% and half the position was sold at $4.94 in early January. Since then, however, the ticker has tumbled. In March and April, we started to buy it back and our average cost is now $2.09.

Investment Thesis

The magnitude of the sell-off this year appears overblown, and as a result, it is once again a solid top pick candidate. In some ways, the rationale today is even stronger than it was in early 2024.

The Corporation's Growth is Set to Accelerate

The company's full year 2024 and first quarter 2025 results were impressive.

Full-year revenues increased from $347.2 million to $394.2 million. As for the bottom line, EPS jumped from $0.07 to $0.27. These figures met the executive team's guidance, and throughout the year the organization met or exceeded consensus analyst estimates.

Though the first quarter saw minimal top-line gains and a minor bottom-line loss, the 2025 outlook is bullish. Management's pro forma calls for sales between $390 million and $430 million, non-GAAP margins of at least 10%, and free cash flow appreciation.

Ceragon's geographic exposure should help drive growth. In the latest quarter, India accounted for 48% of sales, North America 20%, and the rest of the world made up the remainder. This is important because, according to Ericsson's November 2024 industry report, 5G adoption in India stood at around 23% in comparison to North America at 71%. Ericsson estimates India's share will increase to 74% by 2030, while North America will expand to 91%.

India's low 5G adoption and significant runway in the years ahead partly explain why Ceragon's former 2027 goals were pulled forward from an original timeline set in the Q3 2023 earnings release. As a result, in 2026 the CEO expects to produce $500 million in revenue and generate gross margins between 35% and 38%.

As the income statement table above illustrates, the gross margin profile has been below this level over the past 10 years. This combination of expanding gross margins and sales gains is a good sign for owners, as corporations sometimes grow at the expense of margins.

Bolt on Acquisitions, R&D, and New Product Launches

Ceragon's growth prospects can be partly attributed to successful bolt-on purchases and new product launches. In 2023, Ceragon acquired Siklu to increase its footprint in North America's private network market. The relatively small purchase has helped CRNT land new business, and has boosted the organization's overall sales since.

Earlier this year, Ceragon also bought End 2 End Technologies in a further bid to expand its North American private network offering. This transaction looks similar to the one for Siklu, as End 2 End is a small organization and the purchase was cheap. It has already helped them secure $4.1 million in new customer contracts.

In addition to taking over small competitors at relatively low valuations, Ceragon has an impressive product portfolio and is consistently spending tens of millions on R&D annually. In March, for example, the company launched the 2025-Neptune Chipset, which represents a significant leap over the 2013-Jupiter Modem.

The Neptune Chipset is set to enter mass production in the third quarter and follows other new product launches that commenced last year. Management expects to continue this pace of innovation and thinks the new products should perform well in the years ahead.

Relatively Low Tariff Impacts, and Possible Beneficiary of Geopolitical Tensions

Tariffs will have an impact on CRNT, but less so than many firms because the corporation's sales are diversified across the globe. In 2024, the US made up 23% of revenue, while other countries made up the rest. India accounted for 43% of the total, making it by far the company's most important market. This diversification should insulate CRNT from tariffs somewhat.

The raw materials and components used in their devices come from the US, Europe, and Asia. The product assembly occurs in CRNT-owned plants or outsourced facilities in Israel, Singapore, the Philippines, the Netherlands, the US, and China. There may be some shuffling of supply chains if this trade war persists or deepens, but as it stands right now it looks well-diversified.

It is also possible that Ceragon benefits from the geopolitical fallout between the United States and China. As the graphic above demonstrates, Huawei is one of Ceragon's largest competitors. Companies and countries looking to de-risk or reduce exposure to Chinese tech may find Ceragon waiting eagerly to fill the void.

CRNT is an Attractive Acquisition Target

In the past, I have argued CRNT was a potential M&A target. By buying out CRNT, a competitor could add a growing revenue stream to its own business, take a lot of costs out of the combined entity, and boost its bottom line substantially in the process.

In 2022, for example, Aviat Networks (AVNW) twice attempted a tie-up - first at $2.80 in cash, and then at $3.08 in cash. Nothing came of these attempts, and CRNT is now too large for AVNW to buy out in cash. Nevertheless, the rationale for a stock merger between equals is compelling. Alternatively, it could be gobbled up by a much larger player like Ericsson.

This assumes, of course, that the Israeli government would permit such a transaction; the corporation flags Israel's merger laws as a possible impediment to a tie-up.

Valuations Are Low, Insiders Are Aligned, and Financials Are Clean

Ceragon's investment thesis is further underpinned by its low valuations, high insider alignment, and strong financials. It is cheap today versus the market, the sector, and its five-year average. Seeking Alpha's valuation table drives home this point:

Moreover, according to Koyfin, all four analysts covering the name rate it as a Buy. This analyst community's 12-month average price target is $6.13, far above the current level. These factors add weight to the argument that CRNT is undervalued.

As for insiders, Ceragon has a high alignment between insiders and other owners. According to the latest 20-F, the insiders own 10.6%.

CRNT's balance sheet is healthy and has a history of being in good shape. For example, currently the cash balance stands at $27.7 million, the current ratio is under 1.7, and there is only $25.2 million in short-term debt. Sometimes small caps with robust balance sheets are guilty of diluting owners instead of using debt. This is not the case with Ceragon. The share count has inched up over time, but sales and earnings per share have increased more rapidly.

These three features - low valuations, high insider alignment, and sturdy financials - are core ingredients in reducing Ceragon's risk profile, empowering its growth prospects, and providing it with a strong foundation.

Risks

The Growth Strategy Carries Risks

Business growth is preferable to decline or stagnation, but it does come with potential problems. If the margin profile does not improve as the executive team projects, the stock could languish.

M&A integration or overpaying are dangers as well. The experiences with Siklu and End 2 End have gone smoothly and added to the corporation's overall prospects at a reasonable price. This said, acquisitions are hard, and future transactions may not be as successful as these two, especially if the C-suite attempts to down bigger competitors. They must avoid falling into the trap of making bigger and bigger deals where the risk of issues like failing to integrate, realize synergies, or overpaying goes up.

It is Headquartered in Israel

The company is based in Israel. This could be a turnoff for some investors given the ongoing conflict with Hamas, the worsening humanitarian situation in Gaza, and the possibility of conflict with Iran in the future. It should be noted, though, that the hostilities have not impacted the business to date, it has operations around the globe, and nearly all the sales occur outside the country.

Economic or Market Contraction

Ceragon could drop sharply if the stock market tumbles again, the global economy enters recession, or the trade war deepens. Any of these outcomes would be unfortunate, but they would likely serve as buying opportunities. Though investing during a sell-off is not for everyone, it can prove to be a lucrative strategy for the plucky and contrarian investors out there looking to scoop up solid firms on the cheap.

Ceragon is a Small Cap

One final warning - Ceragon is relatively small, with a roughly $200 million market cap. This makes it smaller than many competitors, who could outspend the organization in the future. Its small-cap status also exposes owners to a degree of liquidity risk they would not face owning larger firms. The market cap and liquidity could be a particular impediment to higher institutional ownership.

Conclusion

Ceragon Networks is a top international stock pick thanks to a recent sell-off. The organization benefits from solid growth supported by 5G adoption in its core markets, new product launches, and prudently executed M&A. The company should also be a beneficiary of geopolitical tensions between the United States and China. The trade war impacts should be somewhat muted given that India is Ceragon's most important market, and it could also be an acquisition target. This thesis is not without risks, but the corporation's low valuations, high insider ownership, and clean balance sheet reduce the dangers. Consider Ceragon if you are looking to add international exposure to your portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Civitas Resources: Rare Buying Opportunity With A Hefty Yield
data.description:
Energy remains one of my favorite sectors at this time for bargain hunting on high-yield opportunities. While risks are elevated for this sector due to number of different factors, those with a longer time horizon may be well-positioned to reap substantial benefits on an eventual rebound in the sector.

This brings me to Civitas Resources (NYSE:CIVI), which I last covered in March, highlighting its low-cost structure and robust cash flow generation despite energy price volatility. The stock has fallen by 11% since my last piece, resulting in a compelling valuation yield. In this article, I revisit the stock including recent financial performance and discuss why it remains a ‘Strong Buy’ for investors, so let’s get started!

Why CIVI?

Civitas Resources is a Denver-based independent oil and gas company focused on the development of high-quality and low-breakeven assets in the Permian Basin (specifically the Delaware and Midland sub-basins) and the DJ Basin in Colorado. It emphasizes capital discipline combined with free cash flow generation and shareholder returns.

In fact, management has noted that CIVI is free cash flow positive even at a WTI oil price of $40 per barrel, which sits significantly below the current spot price of $63.81. In a more realistic and still conservative scenario of $60 WTI, CIVI could generate nearly $1.2 billion of FCF, including cost optimization initiatives and a $300 million asset sale, as shown below.

CIVI’s Q1 2025 production volumes were slightly below expectations, but within management’s guidance range. This was due to reduced capital investments at the end of last year and operational disruptions from weather events. Notably, CIVI is improving its capital efficiency by increasing drilling speeds by 10% in the Delaware Basin and completion throughput in the Midland Basin, resulting in a 5% sequential increase in throughput.

Management expects oil throughput to grow by 5% in the second quarter due to growth in the Permian Basin. This momentum is expected to build in the second half of this year, with benefits arising from high till count in the summer of this year. Given near-term uncertainties on the direction of oil prices, CIVI is optimizing costs to unlock an incremental $100 million in free cash flow annually. This is driven by cost efficiencies and productivity enhancements. As shown below, volumes are expected to grow while operating costs are expected to decline due to cost savings initiatives.

In addition, CIVI plans to monetize non-core assets through a $300 million target sale price, and this will help CIVI reach its net debt target of $4.5 billion by year-end. CIVI has a reasonable net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 1.4x and a BB+ crediting rating, sitting just one notch below investment grade.

In the meantime, CIVI maintains strong liquidity and has the ability to sustain its cash flow guidance as 50% of its crude oil production is hedged for the year. Management is reiterated the commitment to a base dividend of $2.00 per share annually, and used FCF to repurchase an impressive 2% of shares during Q1 alone. Over the past 12 months, CIVI has retired 5.9% of its outstanding shares.

Lastly, I believe market sentiment is way too negative on the stock at the current price of $28.51 with a forward PE of 4.7. This sits far below the 15.9 and 17.8 PE ratios of Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), respectively. While I wouldn’t expect for CIVI to trade anywhere near the valuations of large integrated oil giants, I believe the current valuation gap is far too wide. As such, CIVI could deliver substantial returns even if it were to trade at half the valuation of the oil majors. This, combined with the 7% regular yield, makes CIVI a compelling income and value play at present.

Risks to CIVI include its sub-investment grade credit rating, which subjects into higher interest rates. Moreover, the higher reserve decline rate in the DJ Basin compared to the Permian Basin means that CIVI would need to come up with more efficient ways of drilling for oil. Colorado also has stricter environmental regulations compared to Texas, and this could raise CIVI’s compliance costs and/or limit growth in the region.

Investor Takeaway

Civitas Resources presents a compelling high-yield opportunity in the energy sector, trading at a steep discount with a forward P/E of just 4.7 and a 7% dividend yield. Despite a recent production hiccup, CIVI remains fundamentally strong with a low breakeven cost structure and is expected to resume growth in Q2 and the remainder of the year.

CIVI is able to generate robust free cash flow even at conservative oil price assumptions, and has a disciplined approach to capital allocation. Plus, efficiency gains, cost savings initiatives, and robust share repurchases support its long-term value proposition. While regulatory and geological risks exist, the current valuation leaves significant upside potential for patient investors, all while providing a high dividend yield.

data.title: After A Monster Run, American Superconductor Looks Overvalued
data.description:
From one perspective, American Superconductor (NASDAQ:AMSC) stock looks rather questionable. AMSC is a former long-time penny stock and a busted growth play. The company was founded in 1987, went public in late 1991, and even with a five-year rally of 274%, the stock is still down 75% since its IPO.

This is also a stock that doubled in a matter of days in 2023 on a mini-meme rally, when dubious reports of a room temperature superconductor, LK-99, led traders to buy AMSC simply because it had "Superconductor" in its corporate name. The company's previous claim to fame, or infamy, came in 2011, when technology for its wind turbines was brazenly stolen by a Chinese customer. Revenue was crushed, the workforce shrunk, and the stock plunged. This looks like the kind of stock that gets carried higher on broader secular trends (in this case, 'reshoring', data centers, and the need for improvements to the national electricity grid) even if it necessarily shouldn't.

There's clearly a checkered history here, which on its own might suggest some skepticism toward the stock's performance in recent years. But from another perspective, even after that rally, American Superconductor stock looks like potentially the best play on those trends. Shares look impressively cheap: AMSC trades at 45x fiscal 2025 (ending March) adjusted earnings per share, despite revenue growth of 53% in FY25 and guidance for a 60%-plus increase in Q1 FY26. With margin expansion following that revenue growth (adjusted EPS went from $0.02 in FY24 to $0.64 the following year), there seems to be a clear path for profits to grow by multiples, with AMSC shares potentially doing the same.

As is often the case, a closer look suggests the truth is somewhere in the middle between those surface-level bull and bear cases. The bear case no longer really seems to apply: there are very real reasons for optimism here. With AMSC moving back up toward multi-year highs, however, that optimism, and then some, looks priced in.

A Closer Look At Valuation

At $29.86, AMSC trades at 47x FY25 reported non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.64. But that earnings figure is inflated.

To calculate an adjusted net income of just over $24 million, American Superconductor starts with the GAAP net income figure, which is just over $6 million, and then adds back fairly standard non-cash expenses:

But there are three things worth noting here. The first two centers on the GAAP figure itself.

In fiscal 2024, AMSC's operating income in fact was negative. What pushes the company into the black is net interest income of $3.7 million (with a minor offset from currency losses; the two lines alone added $0.09 to EPS) and then a tax benefit of $3.67 million (another $0.095 in EPS). On top of that, the add-backs are all done at an effective tax rate of zero.

This is not necessarily unethical: owing to large net operating loss carryforwards, AMSC isn't paying taxes, and so adjusted EPS for FY25 is accurate in terms of the fiscal year itself. However, it does mean that the adjusted net income and EPS figures skew the perception of the company's profitability. At a standard 21% tax rate, FY25 EPS would have been about one-third lower, with interest income and the exclusion of stock-based compensation combined accounting for more than half of those adjusted profits. In other words, AMSC is trading at closer to 70x normalized tax-adjusted EPS, and a higher multiple when looking solely at the operating business.

Looked at another way, Adjusted EBITDA as usually calculated — $20.75 million, using the GAAP figure and the same add-backs American Superconductor uses — is actually below the adjusted net income AMSC reported for FY25. A stock that looks like it is trading at 47x net income, in fact, on an operating basis, is trading at 55x EBITDA.

How Good Is This Business?

That's obviously a much higher, and much different, multiple. But there is a further consideration. Right now, about a quarter of American Superconductor's EBITDA is coming from the Wind segment (assuming stock-based compensation is assigned to each segment proportional to revenue). And that is not nearly as good of a business.

The Wind segment did post strong growth in FY25 (revenue rose 51%), but the overwhelming majority of sales are to a single customer, India's Inox. (Wind accounted for 16% of AMSC's total revenue, and Inox 14%.) That's dangerous from a customer concentration standpoint, but it's also dangerous because Inox's demand is likely to slow. Inox grew its revenue 88% through the last three quarters of calendar 2024; AMSC's share grew at an apparently slower pace, but it's unlikely Inox can keep growing at 80%-plus for all that long.

A standalone wind business selling into India with a customer concentration of over 80% would not trade at a very high multiple. If we assume even a 15x multiple there, the implied multiple for the Grid segment nears 70x.

When you look closer, AMSC starts to look very expensive. And it's fair to wonder whether this business is quite worth paying up for, at least to this extent. Gross margin was 28% in FY25; Adjusted EBITDA margins were under 10%. Both of those figures will improve thanks to scale and operational fine-tuning, but given that the company is largely an assembler of parts, there almost certainly is a cap on its operating leverage and margins at some point. (To be fair, that manufacturing model does make AMSC quite capital-light: capex was just $2.4 million in FY25, barely 1% of revenue.)

How Fast Is American Superconductor Actually Growing?

At this point, bulls might argue that even if the multiples are higher than headline numbers suggest, there's still a case that AMSC stock is undervalued. After all, the company is growing impressively: again, revenue rose 50%-plus last year and American Superconductor sees a further acceleration in Q1.

But here, too, there are concerns — the most obvious of which is that most of that growth is inorganic. Last year, the company acquired power supplies manufacturer NWL in a deal that cost $61 million. NWL was generating roughly $75 million in revenue at the time, and it's that deal that is responsible for much of the reported top-line increase. Per the 10-K (see p.57), in fact, pro forma revenue in fiscal 2025 rose just 12%.

To be fair, that slow growth is due to the fact that NWL revenue in fact declined by about 1% year-over-year. That's not a huge deal, given that AMSC paid ~12x EBIT (and that disclosures in the K suggest NWL was actually much more profitable in recent quarters, though it's not clear whether non-cash factors are at play there). But even the legacy AMSC business grew revenue only about 18% year-over-year in fiscal 2025.

Bulls might point to orders and backlog, both of which have jumped sharply, but there too NWL has played a key role. So have recent wins with the U.S. and then Royal Canadian Navies — and those contracts (for degaussing systems used for protection from mines) may not necessarily be replicable with other allies.

So in terms of the core business here — the one supposedly benefiting from massive optimism toward data centers and reshoring — there hasn't been quite that much in the way of performance yet. AMSC did have a monster FY24, with revenue up 37% (all of that organic), but that followed a modest decline the year before. It's not hard to wonder if a long-needed and long-awaited turnaround, along with some external help, has driven performance of late — and it's not hard to wonder what the growth rate here will look when the acquisition of NWL is lapped in Q2 and Q3.

The Bull Case For AMSC Stock (Finally)

In that context, it's somewhat surprising that AMSC only has a short interest of about 8% of the float (and the figure now may be even lower given that the stock has doubled off April lows). The combination of a checkered history and a massive rally alongside potentially bubbly trends usually draws bears in. Liquidity isn't an issue either, with a three-month average daily volume at 700,000 shares.

But even with AMSC potentially overvalued on paper, shorting the stock here does not make a lot of sense. The secular trends around reshoring, U.S. semiconductor fab construction, and data centers are all real, and so is the turnaround. There are over $650 million in federal net operating loss carryforwards which have real value, even in an acquisition that would lead to Section 382 limitations. While underlying growth isn't quite what headline numbers suggest, Q1 guidance does suggest an acceleration even on an organic basis, and chief executive officer Daniel McGahn has been exceptionally optimistic in recent quarters.

There's even a potential tariff tailwind, as McGahn detailed on the most recent call:

We have some unique offerings and in some cases, we're the only American provider to be able to provide. So I kind of saw this as the rhetoric is changing after the election that this could present itself as an opportunity for the company.

On the whole, this does not look like a stock to get in front of right now, particularly with the impressive (and continued) strength off April lows. After the turnaround, this is a good company: the naval offering is intriguing, AMSC seems to be taking market share, and the fundamentals are going in the direction that they are supposed to be.

The concern, again, is valuation. It's hard to pin AMSC down fundamentally: other hot (or formerly hot) grid/electrification plays aren't exactly peers, and most of the direct competitors are either private or smaller divisions of much larger public players. But at 50x-plus EBITDA, with organic revenue growth this year likely in the mid- to high-teens, the profile doesn't exactly scream value. There's a lot to like here, but the market may still be giving AMSC too much credit.

data.title: Retire With Long-Term Investment Grade Bonds (Part 4): Globe Life Inc.
data.description:
Today, we will tell you about some of the changes in the past months regarding this baby bond (NYSE:GL.PR.D), since we already wrote about it in January. You can read the previous article and this one and see for yourself where and how the numbers are different, but the most important thing is that the bond continues to be one of our favorites.

Company Overview

Globe Life Inc. (NYSE:GL) has around $30 billion in total assets, $3 billion in total debt, and around $5.4 billion in common equity at the end of the first quarter of 2025. The Total debt/Total asset ratio is around 10%, and the Common Equity/Total Debt ratio is 1.8, which is a good indication for the creditors of the company. Below, we can see the comparison between GL-D and SPY:

Dividends

GL has 35 consecutive years of uninterrupted dividend payments, as the last 9 of them are consecutive dividend increases. Below, you can see the dividend history of the company:

The total dividend paid to GL's common shareholders over the past twelve months is $0.99. The current price of the stock is $121.67, which corresponds to a dividend yield of 0.82%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past five years:

And the forward estimations:

Credit rating

As you know from the previous article, Globe Life is rated 'A-' with a positive outlook by the Fitch credit agency:

Baby bond

GL.PR.D is trading at the moment at $15.48 price with a yield to maturity of 7.52% and years to maturity close to 36. It pays a 4.25% annual interest in the form of $0.2656 quarterly distributions. Globe Life Inc. issued 13 million shares with a $25 par value each of GL-D on 6/9/2021 for gross proceeds of $325 million. It is investment grade, with a credit rating from Moody's: Baa2 and from S&P: BBB+. Its call date is on 06/15/2026.

OTC bonds

On the chart above are the OTC-traded bond issues of GL with their corresponding metrics. The issues are rated Baa1 by Moody's, or a notch higher than the baby bond of the company. We can see that the highest yield is around 5.74%, which is over 2% lower than the yield of GL-D. Therefore, we prefer to invest in a bond with a one-notch lower credit rating but a much higher yield.

Comparison with peers

The charts below contain insurance sector OTC bonds. We can see that the higher yield is 1% below GL-D.

Conclusion

GL.PR.D remains one of our top picks. Despite recent market changes, it continues to offer strong value compared to both OTC bonds and other baby bonds in the insurance sector

data.title: Dividends By The Numbers In May 2025
data.description:
There was bad news for Wall Street's bulls for dividends last month.

May 2025 saw negative developments for dividend-paying stocks in the U.S. stock market. Measured year-over-year, the number of firms acting to cut their dividends rose, and the number of firms announcing dividend increases fell.

Overall, the single number that summarizes the month's dividend changes is -52. This figure represents the net amount by which a reduced number of companies announcing favorable actions like dividend increases or paying extra (or special) dividend payments to their shareholders added to the reduction in the number of firms that either cut or suspended their dividends when compared against their levels in May 2024.

All the month's favorable and unfavorable dividend actions are listed in the following table, which shows how much they changed since May 2024 (year-over-year) and since April 2025 (month-over-month).

Dividend Changes in May 2025
May-2025 Apr-2025 MoM May-2024 YoY
Total Declarations 4,886 3,632 1,254 ▲ 4,419 467 ▲
Favorable 203 159 44 ▲ 245 -42 ▼
- Increases 130 107 23 ▲ 155 -25 ▼
- Special/Extra 72 52 20 ▲ 90 -18 ▼
- Resumed 1 0 1 ▲ 0 1 ▲
Unfavorable 21 4 17 ▲ 11 10 ▲
- Decreases 21 4 17 ▲ 11 10 ▲
- Omitted/Passed 0 0 0 ◀▶ 0 0 ◀▶

The following chart tracks the monthly counts of dividend increases and decreases from January 2004 through May 2025:

The good news is the number of dividend-reducing firms remains well below the threshold that indicates recessionary conditions are present within the U.S. economy.

But the more-than-two-years-old downward trend for dividend increases points to a different kind of continuing stress in the market.

References

data.title: Work Forever? Not Me: 2 Income Machines To Build Your Own Retirement
data.description:
Introduction

I have good and bad news.

Let's start with the good news.

Retirees in the United States just got a big tailwind, as Social Security saw the biggest short-run boost ever. As reported by Bloomberg, the Social Security Fairness Act, which was signed into law on January 5, repealed provisions that had limited/blocked benefits to roughly 3 million people, most of whom are retired public sector workers and others who already received separate government pensions.

According to Social Security Administration data, the number of recipients in current payment status surged by about 812,000 in the three months through April, the biggest such jump on record in data going back to 1967. On a percentage basis, the 1.2% increase in the period was the most since June 2000, with the biggest shift coming in March and affecting checks received in April. - Bloomberg

This has done wonders for personal income. On a nominal and seasonally adjusted basis, personal income jumped by 0.8% in April (compared to March). Government social benefits to persons grew by 2.8%, the highest growth rate since 2006, when excluding pandemic-related benefits.

There are a few things to be aware of here.

For starters, Social Security benefits got one of the biggest tailwinds ever, which supports many retirees and proves that we don't all have to become millionaires in order to avoid financial troubles during retirement.

It also shows that government spending, once again, supports the economy at a time of elevated uncertainty. Since the pandemic, this has been the case, as cyclical headwinds that started in 2021 have been offset by major spending programs like the Inflation Reduction Act and boosts in Social Security.

The headline below fits that narrative:

This brings me to the bad news.

Using the words of Allison Schrager, "America's Debt Problem Is Also a Retirement Problem."

Allison wrote an article shortly after Moody's downgraded America's, noting that America's already unfavorable budget projections do not include unfunded entitlements, which largely consist of Social Security and Medicare.

That's bad news for people who are not yet retired.

For some reason it has become considered politically wise for both Republicans and Democrats to promise they “won’t touch” Social Security. This is not realistic. Within the next 10 years, the program won’t be able to pay full benefits. Something has to change, and that something is the retirement age. Americans like to make fun on the French for their early retirements, but many Americans also retire in their early 60s. In many states, retiring at 62 is the norm. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

While none of us can predict the future, making the case that the retirement age will gradually shift to 70 is a safe bet if you ask me. After roughly six decades of a declining retirement age after the Second World War due to booming economic prosperity and healthy government finances, the retirement age has started to creep up since the 2010s, a trend I expect to gain upside momentum.

Having said all of this, it's not only happening in the U.S.

Most developed nations will see rising retirement ages, with retirement ages in Europe moving to more than 70 for both men and women.

According to the OECD, “based on established links between the retirement age and life expectancy,” the retirement age is projected to increase in 20 countries for men and in 24 countries for women out of the 32 analysed in Europe. Some are also based on already legislated measures.

Future normal retirement ages for those entering the labour market in 2022 are projected to range from 62 to 74 for men, and from 60 to 74 for women by 2060. - Euro News

Current reform plans are expected to shift more retirement ages to the high-60 range, with Italy and the United Kingdom expected to lead the pack.

This comes with a wide range of implications, including that older workers over 60 will be in a tough spot to find a new job if they are laid off before the legal retirement age. Also, some people cannot work until 70. While I get almost all of my work done sitting in front of a computer, I cannot imagine construction workers, nurses, and other people with highly physical jobs working this long.

It also needs to be said that longevity numbers are just average numbers. Wealthy people tend to live longer than less wealthy (poor) people. There are many reasons for this, including access to better food, housing, and healthcare.

People working lower-wage blue-collar jobs are in double jeopardy if retirement ages continue to increase, which puts pressure on governments to make important adjustments.

All of this also puts pressure on every single individual, as future generations will have to take care of their own retirement if they don't want to work 'forever' and become increasingly dependent on a government with debt problems.

At the risk of sounding overly dramatic, I believe it has never been more important for younger generations and everyone not yet retired to focus on building their own retirement accounts.

The future isn't looking bright for people depending on government payments in the future. And, as someone who will get the absolute bare minimum once I retire, this applies to me more than to many others.

It's also why I am always on the hunt to find attractive high-income opportunities, besides the fact that I promised my income-focused investors to provide more food for thought for them.

So, let's dive into two picks that I would trust in an income-focused portfolio for anyone looking to retire early and/or looking to boost their Social Security income.

Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) - One Of My 4 Favorite BDCs Yielding 10%

Elevated yields make me nervous. This is something I often mention, as companies with elevated yields have a higher chance of seeing subdued total returns.

After all, if the market lets a yield remain at high levels (the yield relies on dividend payments and the stock price, which is market-driven), there may be something wrong with a company.

The good news is that Business Development Companies ("BDC") are made for income-focused investors, as they are lending vehicles operating in the private debt industry, a booming place to be due to stricter banking regulations since the Great Financial Crisis.

Interestingly enough, this industry combines two things I dislike:

  • Elevated yields. They make me nervous, as I just mentioned.
  • The lending business is super cyclical, and building a competitive moat is extremely hard. That's why I usually avoid banks.

The good news is that some lenders are fantastic. Personally, I prefer investments in asset managers who own BDCs, as these companies benefit from the rapid growth in assets under management (see the chart above), as I wrote in a recent article.

However, these companies tend to have low yields.

For investors seeking to beef up their income, BDCs are better.

In this case, I like a few, which I have increasingly covered in recent months.

  • Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC)
  • Ares Capital (ARCC)

Both of these BDCs come with high credit quality and support from Blue Owl (OWL) and Ares Management (ARES), two of my favorite asset managers with decades of expertise in private lending and risk management.

Now, I'm adding Blackstone Secured Lending to the mix, a BDC backed by the largest private equity firm in the world, Blackstone (BX), which has more than $1.1 trillion in assets under management, with nearly $380 billion of this coming from alternative assets like private credit.

BXSL's portfolio has a fair value of $12.8 billion, which is 20% more compared to the prior-year quarter. This value comes from loans to 284 companies, after adding 14 new borrowers and exiting six positions in the first quarter of this year. As we can see below, none of its positions accounts for more than 3% of its AUM, with most exposure being tied to loans in the software, healthcare, and professional services industries, accounting for 40% of its AUM.

With regard to risks, the portfolio is heavily weighted toward first lien senior secured loans, which consist of 98% of investments, with 99% of these loans tied to companies backed by financial sponsors. These typically have significant equity cushions, which explains why the average loan-to-value ratio is 47.4%.

To put this in context, the LTV ratio range is usually between 40% and 75%.

But wait. That's not everything.

The portfolio companies have an average last-twelve-months EBITDA of $210 million, with nearly 10% year-over-year EBITDA growth, which is almost double the growth rate of companies in the Lincoln International Private Market database. It also makes them rather large, as many BDCs have average portfolio company sizes in the $100 million range.

Moreover, as of the first quarter, the credit quality of these companies remained strong, with only 0.3% of investments on nonaccrual at cost and 0.1% at fair market value. This is significantly below the BDC peer averages of 2.7% and 1.2%, respectively, according to the company.

One of the reasons why its portfolio companies perform so well is the company's BXCI value creation program, which is used by more than 90% of eligible portfolio companies. This program provides services like group procurement, e-sourcing, and supply chain consulting at no cost to BXSL or the companies.

This is just one of many benefits that come with being backed by Blackstone.

The BDC itself also maintains a pristine balance sheet, with 38% of its drawn debt in unsecured bonds that have a weighted average fixed coupon of less than 3%. Its total weighted average interest rate on its debt is 5.0%.

In 1Q25, the company issued $500 million in net debt at 5.3%. This meant it achieved a spread of 147 basis points over benchmark treasury rates, the tightest spread among all public BDCs this year!

Or to put it differently, it means public markets have a lot of confidence in this BDC, allowing it to borrow debt at very attractive premiums.

So, what about its dividend?

Like most BDCs, it has a very juicy dividend.

In the first quarter, the company maintained its $0.77 dividend. This translates to a yield of 9.7% (let's call it 10%). It's protected by a 108% net investment income payout ratio, which is also one of the highest numbers among peers.

Valuation-wise, we're seeing something that doesn't surprise me, which is that BXSL trades at a 17% premium compared to its book value. This makes sense, as it offers elevated safety for investors, which is rewarded by bond investors through a low spread and by equity investors through an equity premium.

Nonetheless, for income, I don't mind this valuation.

If I were retired, I would likely make high-quality BDCs 15-20% of my portfolio. BXSL would be one of the three I would buy, as it has backing from a fantastic asset manager, a great portfolio, high safety, and a history of outperforming its peers, as we can see below.

The second pick has a lower yield. Yet, it is by no means less impressive.

CME Group (CME) - A Highly Differentiated Financial Giant

CME Group is also part of the financial sector.

However, it's not part of the lending business but a giant in the exchanges industry, as it's the world's leading derivatives marketplace for futures, options, and other derivatives that cover a wide range of asset classes, including interest rates, equities, currencies, agricultural commodities, energy, and metals.

This includes the S&P 500 e-mini, the most liquid futures contract in the world.

Its exchanges include the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (where the company gets its name), the Chicago Board of Trade, the New York Mercantile Exchange, and the Commodity Exchange, also known as COMEX.

In my humble opinion, it's a genius business model, as the company has a wide moat, supported by its Globex electronic trading platform and stakes in S&P Dow Jones Indices, which gives it exclusive licensing agreements.

On top of that, it has a partnership with Google Cloud to optimize its platform and streamline product offerings.

The company also benefits from volatility, as market uncertainty is bullish for volumes. This year, corporations and individual investors are especially looking to hedge, i.e., interest rate risks, causing strong volume growth, as 1Q25 numbers show. As we can see below:

  • 1Q25 revenue was up 10%
  • Total ADVs (average daily volumes) were up 13%
  • Earnings growth was 12%
  • Operating margins were, once again, above 70%.

On top of having anti-cyclical growth capabilities, its margin profile is highly impressive, as the company has very low expenses and the ability to scale its operations with minimal costs and capital expenses.

Because trading is mostly done electronically, the company can add new tools and products rather efficiently, leveraging existing employees and systems. As we can see below, it turned $6.2 billion in revenue into $3.7 billion in net income, turning every revenue dollar into $0.60 of net income, one of the best margins of the entire S&P 500.

As a result, over the past ten years, CME Group has achieved a 7% compound annual growth rate in volume and a 12% CAGR in net income, which has allowed the company to return roughly 360% over the past ten years.

It also remains in a great spot to withstand pressure from new entrants. For example, FMX, which was feared to take away market share, has a share of just 0.1% and an open interest of roughly 7,000 contracts per day. CME does up to 11 million contracts per day, which comes with better liquidity and pricing, benefiting clients and the company itself.

With regard to its dividend, the company is somewhat unorthodox, as it pays four regular quarterly dividends and one special dividend in the first quarter.

  • Currently, it pays a $1.25 quarterly dividend. This translates to a base yield of 1.7%. This dividend aims to return up to 60% of its annual earnings and has been hiked for 14 consecutive years.
  • The variable dividend usually distributes the remaining free cash flow, bringing the total payout to roughly 100%. As the company is expected to boost free cash flow to $4.4 billion by 2027, there's a path to a 4.4% yield on cost, making CME the highest-yielding exchange operator.

Although CME is not a 'traditional' high-yield stock, I would definitely own it in a retirement account (I also own it in my current portfolio) because it offers a decent annualized yield, a fantastic business model that provides protection against market mayhem, an ultra-high margin profile for inflation protection, and the potential for elevated total returns, something most high-yield stocks cannot match.

Moreover, despite rising 24% year-to-date, the company is still attractive.

The company, which has an AA- credit rating, is expected to grow its EPS by mid to high-single digits through at least 2027. Currently, it trades at 27.3x earnings, in line with its two-decade average.

While this may seem like a high multiple, please note that it is warranted because of its high margins, which indicate high-quality earnings.

Adding its dividend, we get a conservative outlook of >10% annual returns, excluding any additional tailwinds from prolonged elevated volatility, which I expect to happen.

Needless to say, I'll continue to expand my position on weakness, as I am convinced CME is a perfect investment for everyone looking to retire at some point in the future or already enjoying retirement and looking to add some differentiated growth for what I expect to be a "higher for longer" volatility environment.

Takeaway

The good news is that retirees just got a massive boost from the Social Security Fairness Act, lifting incomes for millions.

The bad news is that this isn't sustainable.

With unfunded entitlements and rising debt, I believe future retirees, especially younger workers, can't rely on government benefits alone.

The retirement age will more than likely creep up, likely to 70, leaving those in physically demanding jobs vulnerable.

That's why I'm convinced building your own retirement safety net is non-negotiable.

For income-focused investors, I highlighted two picks, which are Blackstone Secured Lending, a high-yield BDC with top-tier backing and great safety, and CME Group, an ultra-efficient exchange operator that comes with both growth and (special) dividends.

While I don't know what the future brings, I know that building income on the side hasn't been this important in a very long time.

Risks To My Thesis

BXSL and CME do not come without risks. While BXSL is a very safe BDC, it is still prone to accelerating defaults if the economy were to enter a steep recession. While CME would benefit in such a scenario from rising volatility, its biggest risk is a prolonged recession, which could cause major market participants to retreat, potentially pressuring volumes.

4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio

My 4-Factor Dividend Growth Strategy is an alternative investment strategy to the popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). You can think of it as having your own custom version of SCHD, tailored to your personal investing style. Let me tell you how it works and the results it has attained thus far.

The 4-factor dividend growth strategy was launched on November 1st, 2022. You can read about the strategy, stock selection process, and portfolio construction in this 4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio article. In a nutshell, the strategy leverages the stock selection process of Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, or rather its underlying index, the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV), with a few minor modifications.

Since I prefer a more growth oriented investment style relative to what SCHD offers, I built my own custom investable universe to which I apply a slightly modified version of SCHD's stock selection process. This is a fully automated process where I do not make any decisions myself.

This portfolio is rebalanced with newly selected stocks on November 1st of each year. You can read more about the most recent rebalancing in this article.

May 2025 Results

The portfolio added +3.19% during the month of May, while the S&P 500 Total Return Index gained +6.29%. SCHD performed worse than SPY and my portfolio last month with a modest gain of +1.36%.

After 7 months, the FY3 return for my portfolio stands at -2.22%, compared to +4.45% for SPY and -5.60% for SCHD.

Since its inception in November 2022, the portfolio has a CAGR of 15.32%, compared to 19.60% for the S&P 500 and 6.22% for SCHD.

Beating the S&P 500 or SCHD is not a primary objective, but it is useful to see how the portfolio measures up against these alternative investments. The goal is for this portfolio to deliver a long-term (5+ years) CAGR of at least 12%. Even though FY3 is not delivering as good of a return as the prior year, the long-term CAGR is holding steady, above my target.

The average gain for all 20 holdings in May was 3.43%. This return differs from the portfolio return due to portfolio drift and the compounding effect since this portfolio is no longer rebalanced on a monthly basis.

Based on analysis of the first two FY, I deemed it more efficient to not rebalance this portfolio each month.

As you can see, the portfolio has fallen behind the S&P 500 largely due to underperformance in November and December 2024. The last 2 months have seen the S&P 500 outperform this portfolio by a wide margin again. The long-term outperformance relative to SCHD remains very high, at 9.09% on an annualized basis. It's worth noting that SCHD has trimmed its loss margin during 2024 but has trailed my portfolio in 2025.

Year-to-date the portfolio is maintaining a modest lead on the S&P 500 and a considerable lead over SCHD.

What I would like to see from this strategy is a strong total return (12% CAGR) over a long period of time, say 5 to 10 years. So far, so good, but we still have a long road ahead of us.

Dividend Review

Currently, the portfolio has a forward dividend yield of 2.28%, which has decreased from the 2.30% dividend yield a month ago. The portfolio generated $2.59 in dividend income during the month of May, the dividends were reinvested in a way to reduce the allocation drift. May 2025 dividend income was 57.68% lower than May 2024 dividend income. FY3 is certainly seeing much more variation in the monthly dividend income relative to prior fiscal years, but it is generating dividend growth relative to both prior fiscal years.

Here is a breakdown of dividend income by fiscal year.

FY1 = $41.29

FY2 = $54.60 (+32.24% increase from FY1)

FY3 = $35.57 (7-months into FY3)

Another way to measure dividend growth is by comparing the projected annual dividend income, or PADI. At the portfolio launch on November 1, 2022, the PADI was $46.79. Clearly, the portfolio did not generate the expected dividend income in FY1, this was primarily due to missing some dividend income in the first month and the timing of monthly rebalancing. Currently, the PADI is $59.61. Comparing the PADI at portfolio launch versus today points to a 27.40% dividend growth. If we annualized this over the 31 months of this portfolio's existence, it equates to a 9.83% dividend CAGR.

In addition to tracking the dividend income and growth of this portfolio, I am also tracking how it compares to the dividend income and growth generated by SCHD over the same time period.

In FY1, an equivalent investment into SCHD, assuming all dividends were reinvested, would have generated $64.63 in dividend income. My portfolio came up 36.11% short of SCHD in terms of dividend income generated.

In FY2, the SCHD equivalent portfolio would have generated $75.09 in dividend income. My portfolio came up 27.29% short of SCHD, while still trailing, this can be seen as an overall improvement.

For perspective, SCHD's dividend growth rate in terms of real dividends paid, between FY1 and FY2, was 16.18% (versus 32.24% for my portfolio).

Given the higher forward dividend yield in my portfolio for FY3, I can hypothesize that the gap in dividend income relative to SCHD will be further trimmed in the upcoming fiscal year.

Thus far in FY3, SCHD is generating 11.74% more dividend income than my portfolio. Granted, my portfolio has one additional month of dividend income relative to SCHD's two already paid dividends. A more apples to apples comparison will be made at the end of July.

4-Factor List For June 2025

Since June 2023, I have been running the 4-factor stock screener on a monthly basis and tracking the list of top-ranked stocks. I want to accumulate this data to run additional tests on more frequent rebalancing and to document how much the list changes from month to month.

Compiling the list is a 2-step process; the first part is generating a shortlist of high-quality dividend growth stocks; the second step is ranking them based on the 4 factors.

Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Biotech Voyager as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

BeOne Medicines Ltd. (NASDAQ:ONC) develops innovative drugs for solid tumors and blood cancers. Particularly, in the chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) field, it has the best-in-class drug zanubrutinib. I believe in the next 5 years, zanubrutinib and its combination therapies will dominate the CLL market with the best efficacy to benefit the patients and bring much return for the long-term shareholders.

Background

BeOne Medicines is a leading biopharma company in the field of oncology. Its main products in the market include zanubrutinib and tislelizumab, respectively, for the hematologic malignancy and solid tumor.

Tislelizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody immunotherapy, which has fierce competition in the worldwide such as Merck's blockbuster drug Keytruda, Imfinzi (AZN) and Opdivo (BMY). Tislelizumab initially was developed in the China market. In China, tislelizumab has been approved for a total of 14 indications, including first-line treatment of gastric or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, ES-SCLC, and neoadjuvant/adjuvant immunotherapy for NSCLC. Outside of China, the company initially partnered with Novartis in 2021with $650 million upfront pay, and then in 2023 Novartis chose to terminate the partnership, which showed the worry of Novartis under the fierce competition of PD-1 market.

Tislelizumab is the cornerstone of BeOne Medicines' solid tumor portfolio, potentially pairing with more than 20 investigational assets as a combination therapy. But the path is not so smooth. Recently, the LAG-3 inhibitor LBL-007 in the phase 2 stage, and the TIGIT inhibitor Ociperlimab in the phase 3 stage were removed from the pipeline. There are still some promising candidates in the concept stage, such as B7-H3 ADC BGB-C354, and PanKRAS inhibitor BGB-53038. I think as the IO combination therapy is the future of immunotherapy, the company's strategy is correct to continuously investigate the possible combinations.

In this report, we will mainly focus on zanubrutinib and the related drug candidates in the field of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), to analyze their competitive positions in the near future.

CLL is a type of blood cancer that originates from the abnormal growth of white blood cells in the bone marrow. Before 2014, chemoimmunotherapy was the standard therapy of CLL, such as fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab (FCR), Bendamustine and Rituximab (BR), or Obinutuzumab and Chlorambucil (OC). Ibrutinib revolutionized the treatment of CLL by offering significant benefits in progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) in previously treated CLL. Ibrutinib is an irreversible Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor, interrupting B-cell receptor signaling pathways and preventing cancer cell survival. As a first-generation BTK inhibitor, ibrutinib had more off-target adverse events, such as atrial fibrillation and hypertension, than BR. To improve the selectivity of BTK inhibition, second-generation BTK inhibitors, such as acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib, was designed and offered more favorable safety profiles compared with ibrutinib. Ibrutinib, acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib all irreversibly and covalently bind to the C481 residue. Another approach is reversible, non-covalent BTK inhibitors, such as Pirtobrutinib. Pirtobrutinib binds to BTK in the ATP-binding region without covalent linkage to C481. The third approach is BTK degraders such as BGB-16673 and NX-5948, which completely eliminate the BTK protein instead of protein inhibition. BTK degraders was deemed to overcome acquired BTK inhibitor resistance mutations. Besides the BTK targeted monotherapies, BTK inhibitor-based combination regimens are also a direction to improve treatment outcomes. In the recent updated NCCN guidelines on CLL, the preferred regimens for first-line treatments include zanubrutinib, acalabrutinib plus obinutuzumab (AO), and venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (VO).

Analysis of Efficacy Data

Ibrutinib, acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib were approved for first-line and post-line CLL treatments. Pirtobrutinib got accelerated approval for adults with CLL who have received at least two prior lines of therapy, including a covalent BTK inhibitor and a BCL-2 inhibitor.

Zanubrutinib was deemed as the best-in-class monotherapy BTK inhibitor in CLL by BeiGene. Acalabrutinib still has a big share with its better tolerability than ibrutinib and its leading position in the first-line combined regimens AO. Pirtobrutinib was deemed as the third-generation BTK inhibitor, and it has several ongoing phase 3 clinical trials either as a first-line treatment, or in a head-to-head comparison with covalent BTK inhibitors. Will zanubrutinib continue its high growth rate, or be replaced gradually by pirtobrutinib in the CLL market in the next 3–5 years? Below, we will try to give some efficacy analysis based on the non-clinical and clinical data.

1. Zanubrutinib versus Ibrutinib

Zanubrutinib is a potent and more selective on-target BTK inhibitor than ibrutinib, designed to minimize off target inhibition of TEC and EGFR family kinases. In the phase 3 ALPINE trial of second-line R/R CLL, zanubrutinib achieved superior PFS over ibrutinib (HR: 0.65 [95% CI, 0.49-0.86] p=0.002). The concerned incidence of atrial fibrillation/flutter events was lower with zanubrutinib vs ibrutinib (7.1% vs 17.0%).

In the first-line untreated CLL, there are no head-to-head trials with ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, but they had respective clinical trials comparing with BR. In the A041202 trial, ibrutinib achieved superior PFS over BR (HR: 0.36 [95% CI, 0.26-0.52] p<0.0001, PFS for patients with BR was 44 months, 48-month PFS rates were ibrutinib 76% vs BR 47%). In the SEQUOIA trial, zanubrutinib achieved superior PFS over BR (HR: 0.29 [95% CI, 0.21-0.40] p<0.0001, median PFS for patients with BR was 44.1 months, 48-month PFS rates were ibrutinib 82% vs BR 48%). Given that the two BR groups had similar PFS results in the trials, it seems that zanubrutinib had better PFS result than ibrutinib in the first-line CLL, but this is not a definite conclusion without head-to-head trial results.

2. Zanubrutinib versus Acalabrutinib

Acalabrutinib is also a second-generation BTK inhibitor with greater selectivity compared with ibrutinib. In the phase 3 head-to-head trial of previously treated CLL, acalabrutinib was noninferior to ibrutinib in terms of PFS (HR: 1.00 [95% CI, 0.79-1.27]). The concerned incidences were lower with acalabrutinib vs ibrutinib, including atrial fibrillation/flutter events (9.4% vs 16.0%), and grade 3 or higher hypertension (4.1% vs 9.1%).

In the first-line ELEVATE-TN trial, acalabrutinib and AO respectively achieved superior PFS over OC (HR: 0.2 [95% CI, 0.13-0.3] P<0.0001, HR: 0.1 [95% CI, 0.06-0.17] p<0.0001, median PFS for patients with OC was 27.8 months, 60-month PFS rates were AO 84%, acalabrutinib 72%, and OC 21%).

Zanubrutinib showed superior efficacy to ibrutinib, while acalabrutinib was only noninferior to ibrutinib. In the first-line trial SEQUOIA and ELEVATE-TN, BR and OC were respectively selected as the comparator, so it is difficult to compare the PFS HR value. But as an indicator, overall response rate (ORR), as assessed by an independent review committee, was 94.6% in zanubrutinib group of SEQUOIA, while ORR was 86% and 94% respectively in acalabrutinib and AO group of ELEVATE-TN. Zanubrutinib showed equal ORR efficacy to AO, and better ORR efficacy than acalabrutinib alone.

3. Zanubrutinib versus Pirtobrutinib

Comparing to zanubrutinib, pirtobrutinib is more selective against kinases such as EGFR/TEC over BTK, and some off-target adverse events such as hypertension occur relatively less infrequently. In the phase 1-2 BRUIN trial, Only 9 of 317 patients (2.8%) discontinued pirtobrutinib owing to treatment-related AE. In the BRUIN CLL-321 phase 3 trial in patients previously treated with a BTK inhibitor, the discontinuation rate due to treatment-related AE was much lower with pirtobrutinib vs BR (5.2% vs.18.3%). Pirtobrutinib has the potential best tolerability and safety among BTK inhibitors, which maybe also bring benefits to the PFS and OS of patients, although additional long-term follow-up is needed to assess the consistency.

Zanubrutinib and pirtobrutinib are both potent BTK inhibitors. In a pharmacokinetics study, zanubrutinib trough concentrations at the recommended dose of 160 mg BID is 7-fold higher than its IC50 concentration. Under the high drug exposure coverage, the median trough BTK occupancy of zanubrutinib is 100% at the 160 mg BID doses, with 93.7% of subjects having >95% occupancy in peripheral blood and 99.6% of subjects in bone marrow. Pirtobrutinib trough concentrations exceeded the BTK IC96 (median 96% BTK occupancy) at the recommended dosage of 200 mg QD. While a direct head-to-head trial is lacking, zanubrutinib may appear to have a slightly higher potency based on its numerical value compared to pirtobrutinib.

Pirtobrutinib demonstrated equal potent inhibition of BTK and BTK C481S. BTK C481S mutation is a known cause of resistance to covalent BTK inhibitors such as zanubrutinib. But overcoming the BTK C481S mutation does not necessarily mean pirtobrutinib will be superior over zanubrutinib because BTK mutations are not necessary for acquired resistance in CLL.

This is in contrast to the cases such as EGFR-mutant lung cancer, which has an activating mutation in EGFR. The third-generation EGFR-TKI osimertinib overcomes the EGFR T790M mutation, which is a common cause of resistance to first and second-generation EGFR-TKIs, and achieved superior efficacy.

The phase 3 ALPINE trial also suggests BTK mutation maybe not be the primary resistance in CLL. From the phase 3 ALPINE trial, 52 patients (24 zanubrutinib-treated, 28 ibrutinib-treated) with progression disease were investigated in the gene mutation analysis at the median follow-up 25.7 months. Only 5 of the 24 zanubrutinib-treated patients have BTK mutations, while only 3 of 28 patients ibrutinib-treated patients have BTK mutations. The results suggest that the mechanisms of early progression on BTK inhibitors may be largely independent of BTK mutations.

Although Pirtobrutinib overcomes the BTK mutation C481S, it got new BTK resistant mutations on V416L, A428D, M437R, T474I, and L528W in the BRUIN trial. In the ALPINE trial, zanubrutinib has resistant mutations on C481S, L528W and A428D.

Now we look at some early existing clinical data of pirtobrutinib. Pirtobrutinib showed its efficacy (ORR 73%) in the BRUIN post-line CLL treatment after a covalent BTK inhibitor and a BCL-2 inhibitor. In the BRUIN CLL-321 phase 3 trial in patients previously treated with a BTK inhibitor, pirtobrutinib achieved superior PFS over chemoimmunotherapy (HR: 0.54 [95% CI, 0.39-0.75], median PFS 14.0 months for the pirtobrutinib arm compared to 8.7 months for the control arm). This evidence shows that pirtobrutinib is a good treatment option for these post-line CLL patients without other effective therapies.

In the first-line CLL, pirtobrutinib was combined with venetoclax and obinutuzumab in a phase 2 trial. At the end of Cycle 7, the u-MRD rates of the combined therapy were 39/43 (91%) in bone marrow and 40/43 (93%) in blood. At the end of Cycle 13, the u-MRD rates of the combined therapy were 26/27 (96%) in bone marrow and 27/27 (93%) in blood. Zanubrutinib was also investigated with venetoclax and obinutuzumab in a first-line CLL. At the end of Cycle 10, the u-MRD rates of the combined therapy were 48/50 (96%) in blood and 46/50 (92%) in both bone marrow and blood. These preliminary data show pirtobrutinib and zanubrutinib both have great potential as combination therapies in the future first-line CLL treatment.

Pirtobrutinib is also being explored as a potential monotherapy in the first-line CLL, including phase 3 trials BRUIN CLL-313 (comparator BR) and BRUIN CLL-314 (comparator ibrutinib). Topline data from the two trials are expected in 2025, which should be big events and redefine the treatment landscape for the CLL field.

The trial BRUIN CLL-313 is similar to the trial A041202 for ibrutinib and SEQUOIA for zanubrutinib. The trial completed enrollment between July 2023 and April 2024. We can estimate from the SEQUOIA trial that the primary endpoint of PFS could be got after about a median follow-up of 2 years for BRUIN CLL-313. The hazard ratio for PFS was 0.39 and 0.36 for the comparison of ibrutinib with BR in A041202, after median follow-up of 38 months and 55 months respectively. The hazard ratio for PFS was 0.4 and 0.29 for the comparison of zanubrutinib with BR in SEQUOIA, after median follow-up of 26.2 months and 61.2 months respectively. Although zanubrutinib is superior to ibrutinib in the second-line R/R CLL, in the first-line trials at the median follow-up of 26.2-38 months, their efficacy is similar. The efficacy of pirtobrutinib at about follow-up of 2 years in BRUIN CLL-313 trial may also be similar, which will show pirtobrutinib is an alternate option of first-line CLL treatment without the evidence of efficacy advantage.

The trial BRUIN CLL-314 will be the first head-to-head clinical trial between BTK inhibitors in the first-line CLL. The trial completed enrollment between July 2023 and April 2024. The primary endpoint of BRUIN CLL-314 is ORR, as assessed by the Independent Review Committee (IRC). As shown in Table 1, ibrutinib achieved ORR 93% in the A041202 trial. If the comparator ibrutinib in the BRUIN CLL-314 shows the same performance, we estimate ORR of pirtobrutinib will be above 97% to reach statistical superiority with 650 patients. Beigene ever failed ASPEN Trial of zanubrutinib compared to ibrutinib for the treatment of patients with waldenström's macroglobulinemia, where the very good partial response (VGPR) is 28.9% vs 19.8% (total patients 164).

The PFS benefit in BRUIN CLL-314 will be more important than ORR for patients. If zanubrutinib compared to ibrutinib based on the data in Table 1, the hazard ratio of zanubrutinib vs ibrutinib is 0.75, at the follow-up of 48 months. This means that the PFS data in BRUIN CLL-314 maybe be got in the year 2027.
